coredata.dc:title: Effects of hydronium and hydroxide ion/group on oxygen reduction reaction electrocatalytic activities of N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Qian
Feng, Jianguang
Liu, Haiying
Xia, Chenhao
Dong, Hongzhou
Sun, Qiong
Yu, Liyan
Dong, Lifeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, density functional theory (DFT) is used to investigate the effects of different forms of H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH on oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalytic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The ORR catalytic activity can be enhanced by ligand functionalization of graphitic N-doped GQDs (gN-GQDs) with H3O and OH. The electrocatalytic activities of different ions/groups on GQDs are different for the presence of ions/groups can rearrange the electronic structure of doped GQDs. The ORR overpotentials of gN-GQDs with H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH are 1.49, 1.80, 0.69, and 0.83 V, respectively. The findings indicate that the adsorption of H3O on the surface of gN-GQDs has the best ORR catalytic performance and can effectively promote oxygen reduction reactions. Analyzing differential electron density illustrates that when H3O is adsorbed to gN-GQDs, the adsorption electron transfer of O2 is more obvious to facilitate the adsorption of O2. The results can reveal the influences of different ions/groups on ORR catalytic mechanisms of GQDs and provide guidance for the design of ORR electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydronium and hydroxide ion/group
Oxygen reduction reaction
Density functional theory

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52002198, 21776147, 21905153 and 61604086), the Qingdao Municipal Science and Technology Bureau (19-6-1-91-nsh), a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J17KA013), and Chemcloudcomputing of National Supercomputing Center in Shenzhen (Shenzhen Cloud Computing Center). L. F. Dong also thanks financial support from the Malmstrom Endowed Fund at Hamline University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of (a) gN-GQD and its surface adsorbed with (b) H3O+, (c) OH–, (d) H3O, and (e) OH. The gray, blue, red and light gray balls present carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and hydrogen atoms, respectively.
Adsorption structures and energies of O2 adsorbed on gN-GQD, gN-H3O+-GQD, gN-OH–-GQD, gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD.
Lowest-energy configurations of OOH, O and OH adsorbed on gN-GQD (a), gN-H3O+-GQD (b), gN-OH–-GQD (c), gN-H3O-GQD (d) and gN-OH-GQD (e). Eads is the adsorption energy.
Free-energy diagrams for the ORR pathway on the surfaces of gN-GQD, gN-H3O+-GQD, gN-OH–-GQD, gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD in acidic medium: (a) U=0 V and (b) Ueq=1.23 V.

Electron density differences of the O2 adsorption on different GQDs: (a) gN-GQD-OO, (b) gN-H3O+-GQD-OO, (c) gN-OH–-GQD-OO, (d) gN-H3O-GQD-OO, and (e) gN-OH-GQD-OO. The yellow color stands for the increase of electron density, and cyan color stands for the loss of electrons. The isovalue is 0.1 a.u.

Density of state (DOS) of (a) pristine gN-GQD, (b) gN-H3O+-GQD, (c) gN-OH–-GQD, (d) gN-H3O-GQD, and (e) gN-OH-GQD. The solid lines represent total DOS (TDOS). The filled regions represent partial DOS (PDOS). The vertical dashed line indicates the position of the HOMO level, and a Gaussian width of 0.02 a.u. is used.

Density of state (DOS) of OO adsorbed on GQDs: (a) gN-GQD-OO; (b) gN-H3O+-GQD-OO; (c) gN-OH–-GQD-OO; (d) gN-H3O-GQD-OO; and (e) gN-OH-GQD-OO. The solid lines represent total DOS (TDOS). The filled regions represent partial DOS (PDOS). The vertical dashed line indicates the position of the HOMO level, and a Gaussian width of 0.02 a.u. is used.
Free energy changes under zero potential for various structures in the five doped GQDs (eV).

head.ce:title: Effects of hydronium and hydroxide ion/group on oxygen reduction reaction electrocatalytic activities of N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facility Horticulture Laboratory of University in Shandong, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Weifang 262700, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Facility Horticulture Laboratory of University in Shandong
Weifang University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFacility Horticulture Laboratory of University in Shandong, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Weifang 262700, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Hamline University, St. Paul 55104, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Physics, Hamline University, St. Paul 55104, USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Q. Guo and J. Feng contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, density functional theory (DFT) is used to investigate the effects of different forms of H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH on oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalytic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The ORR catalytic activity can be enhanced by ligand functionalization of graphitic N-doped GQDs (gN-GQDs) with H3O and OH. The electrocatalytic activities of different ions/groups on GQDs are different for the presence of ions/groups can rearrange the electronic structure of doped GQDs. The ORR overpotentials of gN-GQDs with H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH are 1.49, 1.80, 0.69, and 0.83 V, respectively. The findings indicate that the adsorption of H3O on the surface of gN-GQDs has the best ORR catalytic performance and can effectively promote oxygen reduction reactions. Analyzing differential electron density illustrates that when H3O is adsorbed to gN-GQDs, the adsorption electron transfer of O2 is more obvious to facilitate the adsorption of O2. The results can reveal the influences of different ions/groups on ORR catalytic mechanisms of GQDs and provide guidance for the design of ORR electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The demand for energy resources, such as fossil fuels, has been exacerbated by population growth and industrialization in human society, which is believed to be directly connected to energy crisis and climate changes. Concerns about fossil fuels and the environment have sparked interests in developing new energy technologies [1, 2]. Thus, there is an urge to develop sustainable and environmentally friendly energy conversion and storage systems to avoid an imminent energy crisis in the near future [3]. Recently, electrochemical conversion has attracted substantial interest for renewable energy conversion and storage devices. For example, many researches have been devoted to the development of efficient catalysts for oxygen evolution reaction [4], oxygen reduction reaction [5], carbon monoxide reduction reaction [6], nitrogen reduction reaction [7], among others. Especially, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode is sluggish with high-activation barriers and often limits electrochemical transformation processes in fuel cell systems.

Previous research has demonstrated that the cathodic ORR is five orders of magnitude slower than anodic oxidation reaction, thus ORR is a kinetically limiting component in fuel cells [8], and catalysts are needed to accelerate the ORR kinetics [9–11]. Currently, Pt-based materials are widely employed as the electrocatalysts [12, 13]. However, due to the high cost and limited supply of Pt-based materials, it is essential to develop alternative catalysts to decrease the usage of Pt-based materials [14, 15]. Recently, considerable materials, such as low-Pt [16, 17], non-Pt [18, 19] and even metal-free carbon, have been demonstrated with high activity, long working life and low cost [20, 21]. Remarkably, carbon nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes [22], graphene [23], and graphyne [24] have a great potential as metal-free catalysts to enhance catalytic activities. Moreover, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have also been investigated due to their unique structure-related properties, such as high electronic conductivity and structure tenability at the molecular and morphological levels [25]. The early work on GQDs was dominated by the investigation of their physical properties. More recently, GQDs have been chemically modified and used for applications in energy conversion, bioanalysis, and sensors [26]. It has been conclusively shown that the surface chemical structure of GQDs can play a key role in electro-catalyzing ORR process. For example, nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have abundant edges, good crystal structure, and large surface area, which provide good conductivity and edge active sites for electrochemical and optoelectronic applications [27]. As for ORR catalysts, including N-doped [28, 29], B-doped [30], B/N co-doped [31–33] and S-doped [34, 35] graphene-based materials, have abundant edge features since electrochemically catalyzed reactions occur preferentially at the edge planes rather than the basal plane [36, 37]. Li et al. demonstrated that N-doped colloidal GQDs have highly size-dependent electrocatalytic activities [38]. Qu et al. reported that N-doped GQDs with O-rich functional groups exhibit superb ORR electrocatalytic activities [29]. Dai et al. showed that graphitic N is actually responsible for ORR in three-dimensional interconnected N-doped graphene nanoribbon networks [39]. Some reports suggest that N-doped carbon materials can be utilized for ORR catalysis. Thus, in this study we explore the feasibility of using graphitic N doped GQDs (gN-GQDs) as a catalyst for ORR.

Besides, many aqueous interfacial reactions involve H+ and OH−, and the pH is a key factor for determining their reaction performances in the fields of energy conversion and storage [40–42]. The aqueous hydronium ion, H3O+, is also one of the most functionally important ions for many chemical reactions and processes [43]. Understanding the structure, dynamics, and electronic properties of the hydroxide ion (OH−) has been a subject of many experimental and computational studies [43], and most importantly, functionalization plays an important role in enhancing the molecular properties of compounds [44]. OH– adsorption can determine the O2 reduction reaction pathway [45]. Aqueous solution also impacts the efficiency of the GQD catalysts in many experimental and computational studies [46]. However, the effects of H3O+ and OH– in aqueous solution on oxygen reduction mechanisms have not been systematically explored. As for the ORR, various materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene and GQDs are found to enhance catalytic behavior. There is an urgent need to understand the fundamental role of the hydroxide ion (OH−) and hydronium ions (H3O+) in the GQD intrinsic activity. For comparison to the H3O+ and OH–, we consider gN-GQDs functionalized with hydronium and hydroxyl groups (H3O and OH) as catalysts in order to investigate their ORR catalytic performances using density functional theory (DFT). The present work is designed to analyze the impact of H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH in ORR catalytic activities. The ORR is studied through a four-electron path as the final product is water without any adverse effects on the electrocatalyst surfaces. It is expected that this research can contribute to a deeper understanding of how the H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH affect the ORR, and these findings will help others to design more efficient electrocatalysts.

The DFT calculations were performed using the B3LYP hybrid method in combination with the 6-311G (d, p) [47, 48] basis set in Gaussian 09 [49]. The standard convergence criteria set by the software are applied in all geometry optimizations, which are 0.00045 Hartree/Bohr and 0.0003 Hartree/Bohr for the maximum and root mean square (RMS) forces, respectively, and 0.0018 and 0.0012 Bohr for the maximum and RMS displacements, respectively. Spin-unrestricted calculations were used to consider spin polarization effects [50], and the contribution of bulk solvent (water) to the Gibbs free energy was computed using the polarizable continuum model [51, 52]. As shown in Fig. 1 , in this study, 46 carbon atoms and one graphitic nitrogen atom (C46N) are constructed with four different ions/groups: H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH.

The adsorption GQD architectures were prepared by adsorbing one ion/group onto the gN-GQD surface: H3O+, OH–, H3O, and OH, denoted as gN-X-GQD (X= H3O+, OH–, H3O, and OH). The formation energy (
E f
) was calculated to investigate the stability of adsorbed gN-GQD,
(1)
E f
=
E
g N − X − G Q D
−
E
g N − G Q D
−
E X
where,
E
g N − X − G Q D
,
E
g N − G Q D
and
E X
are the total energy of gN-X-GQD, the energy of gN-GQD and the energy of adsorption ions/groups X, respectively. The negative value of
E f
corresponds to a thermodynamic stable composite.

The adsorption energy (
E
a d s
) of the adsorbates, such as gN-GQD and gN-X-GQD with O2, OOH, O and OH, was estimated as the following equations,
(2)
E
a d s
=
E
a d s o r b a t e @ g N − G Q D
−
E
g N − G Q D
−
E
a d s o r b a t e
(3)
E
a d s
=
E
a d s o r b a t e @ g N − X − G Q D
−
E
g N − X − G Q D
−
E
a d s o r b a t e
where,
E
a d s o r b a t e @ g N − G Q D
,
E
a d s o r b a t e @ g N − X − G Q D
,
E
g N − G Q D
,
E
g N − X − G Q D
and
E
a d s o r b a t e
represent the total energy of the gN-GQD together with the adsorbate, the gN-X-GQD together with the adsorbate, an isolated gN-GQD, an isolated gN-X-GQD, and an adsorbate, respectively. The negative value indicates a favorable exothermic adsorption.

The ORR can proceed through the following elementary steps usually employed to investigate the ORR electrocatalysis on various materials:
(4)
* +
O 2
+
H +
+
e −
→ * O O H
(5)
* O O H +
H +
+
e −
→ * O +
H 2
O
(6)
* O +
H 2
O +
H +
+
e −
→ * O H +
H 2
O
(7)
* O H +
H 2
O +
H +
+
e −
→ * + 2
H 2
O
where * stands for an active site on the surface of electrocatalyst, and *OOH, *O and *OH are the adsorbed intermediates.

The free energy diagrams of ORR can be calculated based on the method proposed by Nørskov et al [53],
(8)
Δ
G =
Δ
E +
Δ
Z P E − T
Δ
S +
Δ
G U
+
Δ
G
p H
where
Δ
E
is energy change in the reaction,
Δ
Z P E
is zero-point energy calculated, T is the temperature, and
Δ
S
is the entropy change. The
Δ
G U
is defined as –neU, where n, e and U are the number of proton-electron transferred pairs, transferred charge and the electrode applied potential, respectively.
Δ
G
p H
= −
k B
T ln
[
H +
]
= p H ×
k B
T
ln 10
where
k B
is the Boltzmann constant and T is the temperature.

Various studies have stated that the potential region of a 4e− reduction coincides with the presence of specifically adsorbed OH− anions on the surface, while the absence of OH− causes the reduction to occur through a 2e− pathway. Thus, the O2 reduction reaction pathway may be affected by OH– adsorption [45]. So, it is necessary to understand the fundamental role of the hydroxide ion (OH−) and hydronium ions (H3O+) in the GQD intrinsic activity. In order to establish a comparison and better explain the effects of functionalization on oxygen reduction reaction mechanisms, the adsorption of H3O+ is also considered. Fig. 1 exhibit four adsorption structures, including gN-H3O+-GQD, gN-OH–-GQD, gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD, which represent the structures of H3O+, OH–, H3O, and OH absorbed on graphitic N-doped GQDs. The coordinates of the atoms in N-doped GQDs with four different ions/groups are listed in Table S1–S5 of supporting information (SI). As shown in Fig. 1a, the planar structure of GQD is retained through N doping. The H3O+ and OH– have a distance from GQDs and the planar structure of GQDs is retained (Fig.1b and 1c), however, the H3O and OH can form bonds with C atom on GQDs, which causes carbon atoms to move out of the gN-GQD plane to form a tetrahedral structure and changes the geometric configuration of GQDs (Fig.1d and 1e). It can be clearly seen that the H in H3O forms a bond with the C atom and a hydrogen bond (2.51 Å) with O atom, which is consistent with other reports [54, 55]. Thus, H3O+ and OH– bonds with or without the surface have a great influence on the surface structure of GQDs. To evaluate the stability of the four adsorption structures, their formation energies are calculated. All are negative, which indicates that the four structures are easy to form, and their stability is in the order of gN-H3O-GQD > gN-OH-GQD > gN-OH–-GQD > gN-H3O+-GQD according to the formation energy (–1.51 < –1.20 < –0.61 < –0.54 eV). It implies that the gN-H3O-GQD is the most energetically favorable and stable.

The electronic structures of the GQDs with different ions/groups were determined and their effects on catalytic properties were analyzed. The presence of hydronium ion and hydroxide ion as well as the functionalization of H3O and OH can rearrange the charge and spin density of doped GQDs, and the stable structures of gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs, gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs are obtained through energy minimization (Fig. S1–S3). As demonstrated in our previous study [56], the carbon atoms with high spin density or high charge density are most likely to be catalytic active sites. The charge and spin density of the GQDs with different absorptions were calculated and are shown in Table S6–S8 of SI, and the atoms with higher charge density or higher spin density are respectively marked by red circles and blue dotted circles in Fig. S1–S3. For the five structures, the atoms with higher charge density are located at similar positions (e.g., C2, C4, C5, C16, C25, C31). However, the atoms with higher spin density on gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD are located at zigzag edge (Fig. S3 and Table S8). It was reported that spin density is much more important in determining the catalytic active sites [56]. It can be clearly seen that the presence or adsorption of different ions/groups can affect the spin density distribution of gN-GQDs. For gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD, the O2 molecule tends to be adsorbed at locations with higher spin density. Hence, different ions/groups surrounding or directly attached to GQDs can lead to high asymmetric distribution of spin density and atomic charge density and thereby, affect the ORR electrocatalytic activities.

The adsorption of O2 on the catalytic active sites is the first decisive step for a material to show ORR catalytic capability. To examine catalytic capability of the five different GQD surfaces, O2 molecule is introduced over the atoms with high spin or high charge density in a distance of around 1.5 Å. Then, the adsorbates are relaxed from various initial structures, and the adsorption energy corresponding to the most energetically favorable configuration of O2 on the five studied structures is shown in Fig. 2 . Oxygen adsorption is a complex process in ORR, which can be physisorption, chemisorption, or dissociative chemisorption in the electrochemical reaction process. For example, Ma et al. investigated the differences between optimized chemisorption and physisorption configurations for O2 molecules on phosphorus doped-C9N4 monolayers. The bond length of O–O in the physisorption configurations was circa 1.25 Å, while the O–O bond length was around 1.50 Å in the chemisorption configuration with the adsorption energy of 0.62 eV [57]. As shown in Fig. 2, O2 is adsorbed on the surfaces of gN-GQDs, gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs, gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs with an O−O bond length of 1.42 Å, 1.42 Å, 1.57 Å, 1.44 Å and 1.47 Å, which indicates that the bond length of O=O is largely affected during geometry optimization as compared to the O2 bond length of 1.23 Å. Hence, O2 adsorption is chemisorption on the GQD plane prior to the ORR process. After full configuration optimization, the relative stability of the optimized structures was determined by calculating the adsorption energies. As given in Fig. 2, the stability of O2 on gN-GQD, gN-H3O+-GQD, gN-OH–-GQD and gN-OH-GQD requires higher energy than gN-H3O-GQD, which indicates that O2 adsorbed on gN-H3O-GQD has more thermodynamically favorable configuration than the others.

The 4e– pathway of ORR on GQDs is assumed to proceed through an associative mechanism, which initiates via the adsorption of molecular O2 on the surface and follows reductive hydrogenation steps to form *OOH, *O and *OH. As shown in Fig. 3 , after the O2 molecule is adsorbed on the GQD surface, the *O2 molecule may seize a proton to form an *OOH species. After the first electron transfer, subsequent electron transfer reactions are determined by introducing protons into the system. When a proton is introduced near the adsorbed OOH, it bonds to an oxygen atom, which results in the breaking of O-O bond and the formation of one water molecule while the other oxygen atom still adsorbs on the GQDs to form *O. Then, the resulting *O can be further reduced to *OH. The adsorption energies for the dominated ORR intermediates on gN-GQDs, gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs, gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs are also given in Fig. 3. The negative adsorption energy means that the adsorption process is exothermic and that all the intermediates are easily adsorbed on the surfaces.

The free energy diagrams of the ORR on gN-GQDs, gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs, gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs are explored to compare their associative pathways (Fig. 4 ). In Fig. 4a, the free energy diagram shows the reduction pathway of O2 hydrogenation to form H2O under zero potential, and the details of the free energy changes under zero potential for the five doped GQDs can be seen in Table 1 . It can be clearly found that gN-GQD and gN-H3O+-GQD have similar catalytic abilities: The first electron transfer is an endothermic reaction and the others are exothermic reactions. However, for gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs, all the fundamental reactions are exothermic reactions under zero potential, which indicates that the functionalization of H3O and OH can greatly promote the oxygen reduction reaction. Hence, it can be clearly found that catalytic activity sequence is as follows: gN-H3O-GQDs > gN-OH-GQDs > gN-GQDs > gN-H3O+-GQDs > gN-OH–-GQDs. Considering cathode electrocatalysts in ORR usually operate under positive electrode potentials in practical applications, the influence of different external potentials (U) on the ORR pathways is investigated. For the ORR reaction, the onset potential is calculated by Eq. 9 [53], while the overpotential is the potential obtained by Eq. 10 [53, 58, 59]
(9)
U
O R R
= − max
{
Δ
G 1
,
Δ
G 2
,
Δ
G 3
,
Δ
G 4
}
/ e
(10)
η
O R R
= 1.23
V −
U
O R R
where
Δ
G 1
,
Δ
G 2
,
Δ
G 3
and
Δ
G 4
are the Gibbs free energies of the four reaction steps given by equations (4)–(7); and 1.23 V is defined as the equilibrium potential of the overall 4e– ORR for pH=0 at T= 298.15 K. Since most of elementary steps are downhill at U = 0 V, implying a facile reaction. The reaction steps are uphill as the applied potential U is changed from 0 to 1.23 V in acidic conditions. Hence, the reaction at equilibrium potential exhibits a maximum free energy change between the adjacent steps allowed by thermodynamics, which is the rate determination step (RDS). An overpotential is required to overcome the positive free energy change, which at least equals to the rate-limiting step of the free reaction energies. The overpotential
η
O R R
is widely used to evaluate the ORR catalytic activities of active sites and can be calculated through plotting the reaction free-energy diagram (Fig. 4b) at the equilibrium potential (Ueq = 1.23 V). The
η
O R R
is indicated by the arrow. It can be seen that for gN-H3O+-GQD and gN-OH–-GQD, the
η
O R R
can be high as 1.49 V and 1.80 V, which suggests poor ORR activities. On the other hand, gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD can respectively reduce
η
O R R
to 0.69 V and 0.83 V, significantly improving the ORR performances. Taking gN-H3O-GQD as an example (Fig. 4b), the diagram indicates that the first electron transfer step to form chemisorbed *OOH is an endothermic reaction with a free energy change ΔG1 (Ueq) = 0.69 eV, while the second step is an exothermic reaction with ΔG2 (Ueq) = −1.02 eV and the third and fourth steps are endothermic reactions with ΔG3 (Ueq) = 0.06 eV and ΔG4 (Ueq) = 0.27 eV, respectively. Especially, the ΔG1 (Ueq) is the largest endothermic step among the free energy changes of all four reaction steps, which implies that this step is difficult to form *OOH and this step is the RDS for the whole ORR. We observe that in addition to the initial barrier at step 1 discussed above, gN-GQDs, gN-H3O+-GQDs, and gN-OH–-GQDs need to overcome another barrier during the step of
* O → * O H
. Furthermore, gN-H3O-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs need to overcome a barrier at the last step where the second water molecule is formed. For gN-GQDs, gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs, the RDS is also the first step to form *OOH. In comparison, the lower
η
O R R
values of gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD indicate that they have better ORR catalytic ability, and gN-H3O-GQD is the most effective and stable structure for ORR. Thus, we conclude that the gN-H3O-GQD is most likely the stability structure for ORR. This rather intriguing finding could be attributed to different interactions between H3O and gN-GOQ. As described above, the H atom in H3O forms a bond with the C atom and a hydrogen bond with O atom, which is a joint contribution of chemical bonding and electrostatic interactions. However, for gN-H3O+-GQDs, gN-OH–-GQDs and gN-OH-GQDs, there is only one form of chemical bonding or Van der Waals interaction between adsorbate and GQDs. In gN-H3O-GQD, the chemical bonding of H in H3O to an sp2-bonded C atom requires a local rehybridization from sp2 to sp3, and hydrogen bond is formed as the electrostatic interaction between the HOH donor and H acceptor atoms in H3O. The findings further indicate that the existence of sp3-hybridization C atom and hydrogen bond could enhance ORR catalytic ability.

It is well known that the physical and chemical properties of GQDs are size dependent. Hence, graphitic N doped GQDs containing 36 (C36N), 42 (C42N), 52 (C52N) and 56 (C56N) C atoms are selected to reveal the mechanisms underlying the ORR of GQDs with different sizes. In addition, the H3O group is chosen to study the catalytic trend of GQDs. The optimized structures of H3O-GQD are obtained based on energy minimizations as shown in Fig. S4, and the free-energy diagrams for the ORR pathway under Ueq=1.23 V are given in Fig. S5. The calculated hydrogen bonds are about 2.54 Å when the H3O absorbs on different GQDs (C36N, C42N, C52N and C56N), which are consistent with earlier research results. The active sites for the ORR with different GQDs are also determined according to the distribution of spin density. The details on free energy changes under equilibrium potential for gN-H3O-GQD with different carbon atoms can be seen in Table S9. As show in Fig. S5, the ORR catalytic trend of H3O-GQDs is variously changed with the GQD size. It can be clearly found that gN-H3O-GQDs with different GQD size have similar catalytic abilities except for gN-H3O-GQD(C36N). The first electron transfer of gN-H3O-GQD(C36N) is an exothermic reaction and the others are endothermic reactions. Based on the overpotential value in Table S9, it can be clearly found that the ORR catalytic ability is enhanced as the number of carbon atoms of the GQD increases. However, the RDS of C36N and C42N is *OH+H2O+H++e–→*+2H2O and the other three GQD is *+O2+H++e–→*OOH. This finding may be explained by the fact that the GQDs containing different carbon numbers have different sizes and edges.

The contribution of pH value has an important role on ORR activity. However, the pH effect is very hard to be considered directly in the molecular level quantum mechanical description of the entire surface-system interactions. Generally, the pH dependent is investigated using the method proposed by Nørskov et al [53]. The effect of a pH different from 0 can correct the free energy of H+ (
Δ
G
p H
) ions by the concentration dependence of the entropy. Hence, the free-energy diagrams for the ORR pathway of gN-H3O-GQD with different pH (pH=1, 3 and 5) in acidic medium under equilibrium potential are calculated to understand the dependence of pH on the electrocatalytic ORR (Fig. S6 and Table S10). As shown in Fig. S6, the changes of ORR pathway with different pH have similar catalytic abilities, and the energy of ORR pathway is reduced as the pH value increases. It can be seen from free energy changes in Table S10 that free energy change of each proton and electron transfer step is increased as the pH value increases. This result means that the ORR reactions need to overcome higher energy barrier as the concentration of H+ is reduced.

The formation of chemical bond always leads to significant electron reorganization (polarization and charge transfer), especially the formation of covalent bond is accompanied with the phenomenon that electrons concentrate to the bonding region. To obtain more information on the interface property, plotting electron density difference (EDD) is one of the best ways to reveal this point. Considering oxygen chemisorption is an important dissociative adsorption in electrochemical reaction process, the electron density difference of the O2/GQD complex is examined to comprehend the nature of the O2/GQD bonding characteristics. The electron density change during the adsorption process Δρ is defined as:
(11)
Δ
ρ
= ρ
(
O 2
/ GQDs
)
− ρ
( GQDs )
− ρ
(
O 2
)
where
ρ (
O 2
/ GQDs
)
,
ρ
( GQDs )
and
ρ (
O 2
)
are the electron density of GQDs adsorbed system, GQDs and an isolated O2 molecule for the adsorbed system, respectively. The isosurfaces for the electron density difference are plotted in Fig. 5 . The yellow represents the increase of electron density, and cyan indicates the loss of electrons. Evidently, some electrons accumulate around the O2 molecule, which indicates the binding between O2 and GQDs is induced by adsorption and beneficial to the reduction of O2. There is significant enrichment intermediate from “O” and “C”, indicating an obvious charge transfer from GQDs to O2 molecule. These electron density redistributions in the O2/GQD complexes indicates a significant interaction between O2 and GQDs. The electron density differences of the O2 adsorption on gN-GQD and gN-H3O+-GQD (Fig. 5a and 5b) are similar, indicating that the adsorption capacity of O2 on these two surfaces is similar, which is consistent with the above analysis that the adsorption energies of oxygen on gN-GQD and gN-H3O+-GQD are similar (Fig. 2). What's more, the electron density differences of the O2 adsorption on gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD (Fig. 5d and 5e) are also similar, which can explain that the adsorption energies of O2 on gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD are similar. In addition, the electron transfer ability of gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD exhibits more symmetrical than those of gN-H3O+-GQD and gN-OH–-GQD. This indicates that the groups attached on gN-GQD surface can more effectively enhance the electron transfer of GQDs than those ions surrounding the gN-GQD surfaces.

To have a deeper insight into the effect of O2 adsorption on the electronic structure of GQDs, the total density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of the GQDs without and with adsorbed O2 were calculated (Fig. 6 and 7
). The plots of TDOS and PDOS provide a pictorial representation of molecular orbital compositions and the contributions of fragments in the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6, the solid lines represent the total DOS (TDOS); the filled regions represent the partial DOS (PDOS) of the H3O+, OH–, H3O and OH in graphitic N doped GQDs, respectively. Compared with the TDOS of pristine GQDs, the variations of the other four GQDs with ions/groups are evident. This implies that there is a significant role of different ions/groups, which can alter the orbital characters of GQDs. What's more, the PDOS of different ions/groups in Fig. 6 indicates that they play a prominent role in tuning the electronic structure of N doped GQDs. As given in Fig. 7, the electron-rich O2 molecule interacts with the GQD surface, and their electron densities are largely overlapped. There is also a notable difference among the PDOS of O2 due to its different adsorption modes on different GQD surfaces. In comparison, the intensity of the PDOS for the contributions of O2 is similar when oxygen adsorbs on the structures of gN-GQD and gN-H3O+-GQD (Fig. 7a and 7b), which is consistent with the above results of oxygen adsorption energy and electron transfer on the two surfaces. Similarly, the intensity of the PDOS for the contributions of O2 is similar when oxygen adsorbs on the structures of gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD (Fig. 7d and 7e). Similar to gN-GQD-OO, the PDOS of O2 in gN-H3O+-GQD-OO and gN-OH–-GQD-OO has a notable peak at the HOMO energy level, which can be because the ions existing on the gN-GQD surface maintain their sp2 hybridization and planar structure. While H3O and OH adsorb on the GQD surface to form functional groups, they cause more structure distortion in gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD. This suggests that the presence of neutral species, such as H3O and OH groups, can change electronic structures for gN-GQDs and thereby promote the ORR catalytic performance. Importantly, the adsorption energy, overpotential values and electron transfer indicate that OH and H3O functionalization has good response for catalytic applications, which reveal that gN-H3O-GQD and gN-OH-GQD have favorable ORR catalytic activity.

In this work, we analyze how different ions/groups absorbed on the GQD surfaces can affect the ORR catalytic properties in terms of the formation energy, Gibbs free energy, and electronic properties. The formation energy implies that the four absorption structures: gN-H3O+-GQD, gN-OH–-GQD, gN-H3O-GQD, and gN-OH-GQD, are energetically favorable and stable. Especially, the gN-H3O-GQD is the most energetically favorable and stable. In addition, the introduction of different ions/groups can affect electronic properties, especially spin and charge density distributions. Importantly, the adsorption energy, overpotential values and electron transfer indicate that OH and H3O functionalization has good catalytic activity, and the gN-H3O-GQD is a desirable structure for ORR due to the most effective and stable structure, which suggests that regulating the electronic structure by functionalization on the GQD surface can improve the catalytic performance of oxygen reduction reaction. It anticipates that this study will provide useful information in designing efficient GQD catalysts for electrochemical reaction process.

Qian Guo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Jianguang Feng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Haiying Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Chenhao Xia: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hongzhou Dong: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Qiong Sun: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Liyan Yu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Lifeng Dong: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52002198, 21776147, 21905153 and 61604086), the Qingdao Municipal Science and Technology Bureau (19-6-1-91-nsh), a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J17KA013), and Chemcloudcomputing of National Supercomputing Center in Shenzhen (Shenzhen Cloud Computing Center). L. F. Dong also thanks financial support from the Malmstrom Endowed Fund at Hamline University.
All data used to generate the results in this paper can be obtained from the corresponding authors upon reasonable request.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2021.112009.

J. Tomasi, B. Mennucci, R. Cammi, Quantum mechanical continuum solvation models, Chem. Rev.105 (2005) 2999–3094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Universal solvation model based on solute electron density and on a continuum model of the solvent defined by the bulk dielectric constant and atomic surface tensions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.V. Marenich, C.J. Cramer, D.G. Truhlar, Universal solvation model based on solute electron density and on a continuum model of the solvent defined by the bulk dielectric constant and atomic surface tensions, J. Phys. Chem. B113 (2009) 6378–6396.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origin of the overpotential for oxygen reduction at a fuel-cell cathode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.K. Nørskov, J. Rossmeisl, A. Logadottir, L. Lindqvist, J.R. Kitchin, T. Bligaard, H. Jonsson, Origin of the overpotential for oxygen reduction at a fuel-cell cathode, J. Phys. Chem. B108 (2004) 17886–17892.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The nature of hydrogen bonds: A delineation of the role of different energy components on hydrogen bond strengths and lengths

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.C. van der Lubbe, C. Fonseca Guerra, The nature of hydrogen bonds: A delineation of the role of different energy components on hydrogen bond strengths and lengths, Chem. Asian J.14 (2019) 2760–2769.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An empirical correlation between stretching vibration redshift and hydrogen bond length

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Rozenberg, A. Loewenschuss, Y. Marcus, An empirical correlation between stretching vibration redshift and hydrogen bond length, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys.2 (2000) 2699–2702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical insights into enhanced electrocatalytic activity of oxygen reduction reactions on N/S-Codoped Graphene Quantum Dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Guo, J. Feng, D. Chen, N. Song, H. Dong, L. Yu, L. Dong, Theoretical insights into enhanced electrocatalytic activity of oxygen reduction reactions on N/S-Codoped Graphene Quantum Dots, J. Phys. Chem. C125 (2021) 9747–9755.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First-principles study of the oxygen reduction reaction on the boron-doped C9N4 metal-free catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. He, J. Shen, Z. Lu, D. Ma, First-principles study of the oxygen reduction reaction on the boron-doped C9N4 metal-free catalyst, Appl. Surf. Sci.527 (2020) 146828.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of active sites in N-coordinated Fe-Co dual-metal doped graphene for oxygen reduction and evolution reactions: a theoretical insight

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding catalytic activity trends in the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kulkarni, S. Siahrostami, A. Patel, J.K. Nørskov, Understanding catalytic activity trends in the oxygen reduction reaction, Chem. Rev.118 (2018) 2302–2312.
